title: IT (mild spoilers) - The Ran(king) of King - Part 9
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=01F_B8RaurQ

Daniel (@dungeondive) tackles one of King's most famous works in The Ran(King) of King — 1986's IT, on his third read and loving it more than ever. An ancient alien evil feeds on the town of Derry, Maine; seven misfit kids, the Losers' Club, confront it as children and must return as adults to finish the job. He reads passages aloud to showcase some of King's finest writing.

He singles out IT as the supreme example of King's mastery of structure — a thousand-plus pages mapped by a detailed table of contents, weaving between 1958 and 1985, between the kids' and adults' viewpoints, the town's history (via librarian Mike Hanlon's interludes), and IT's own cosmic perspective. Pennywise the clown and the Universal-monster forms do little for him personally; what fascinates him is IT as an ancient cosmic force that compels adults to forget and look away — and the childhood-bully villain Henry Bowers, a King "lieutenant" more terrifying than the big bad. The book's slightly unfocused theming is offset by its real core: the profound, all-forms love and friendship of the Losers.

On the board it lands just beneath the more laser-focused Pet Sematary.

----

Is IT's cosmic, adults-forget horror scarier to you than Pennywise — and does its sprawling theming help or hurt the book?
